title: Fork, exec, and zombie reaping
description: fork duplicates the process and exec replaces its image; a child that exits stays a zombie until the parent waits for it.
body: Use when spawning subprocesses. fork() creates a child copy of the process (copy-on-write memory, duplicated fds), and exec() replaces the child's image with a new program; the child inherits open fds unless they are marked close-on-exec. When a child exits, the kernel keeps its exit status as a zombie entry until the parent reaps it with wait/waitpid; failing to reap leaks process-table slots. Do not ignore SIGCHLD or forget to wait. Actions: set CLOEXEC on fds you do not want inherited, reap children in a loop (waitpid with WNOHANG), and handle SIGCHLD safely. Validation signals include no accumulating zombie/defunct processes and children not inheriting stray fds. Risk boundary: leaking fds into children can expose secrets or hold resources; leaking zombies eventually exhausts the process table.
